mL8ThkuaVTM.root_cause_summary: Candidate_2's hysteresis patch regressed a previously successful episode by altering the stair approach mode: candidate_0 traversed stairs passively via climb_stair(91-102) and found the toilet (SUCCESS, DTG~0.04), while candidate_2's patch diverted the agent into an oscillating get_close_to_stair(118-128)→look_for_downstair(129-135)→get_close_to_stair(136-140) cycle that inflated floor_reinits from 3 to 6 and exhausted 499 steps. The failure tag 'feasible' confirms the toilet IS 2D-connected to the agent — the regression is caused by the patch disrupting the passive climb_stair path, not by floor confusion.
key_evidence: candidate_0 SUCCESS at step 312: climb_stair(91-102) with floor_step reset to 0 at step 287; floor_step=13 at step 300 confirms sustained floor-2 exploration; DTG avg ~0.04
candidate_2 FAIL at step 499: get_close_to_stair(118-128)→look_for_downstair(129-135)→get_close_to_stair(136-140) oscillation begins at step 118; reinits=6 vs 3 in candidate_0
Failure tag 'never_saw_target_traveled_stairs_feasible': toilet IS in same 2D connected region as agent start; this is not a cross-floor or navmesh-disconnection failure
Running avg DTG at episode 3 for candidate_2: 1.4607 (~4.38m estimated per-episode vs ~0 for candidate_0 success)
Behavioral fingerprints differ substantially (not identical), confirming patch changed the execution path — unlike XB4GS9ShBRE and qyAac8rV8Zk where patches had zero effect

candidate_outcomes.candidate_0: SUCCESS in 312 steps: passive climb_stair(91-102) traversed stairs at step 287 (floor_step reset to 0); explored floor 2 (floor_step=13 at step 300); found toilet
candidate_outcomes.candidate_2: FAIL in 499 steps: hysteresis patch changed stair approach to get_close_to_stair→look_for_downstair oscillation from step 118; 6 reinits vs 3; toilet never found despite 2D feasibility

ruled_out_reasoning.DP12_increase_to_80: Raising DP12 was intended to reduce floor-switch frequency; instead it changed the stair approach mode from passive climb_stair to active get_close_to_stair, increasing reinits from 3 to 6 — the opposite of the intended effect
ruled_out_reasoning.floor_confusion_hypothesis: mL8ThkuaVTM was NOT a floor_confusion failure in Track 4: candidate_0 solved it cleanly in 312 steps with passive stair detection; the CLAUDE.md floor_confusion classification did not apply to the Track 4 baseline
ruled_out_reasoning.LLM_DP7: dp7_empty=0/0 for both candidates; LLM calls either absent or perfectly parsed; LLM path is not causal here
ruled_out_reasoning.DP1: DP1 frontier scores in candidate_2 logs show normal proximity-boosted operation; frontier scoring is not the gating factor for the stair approach mode switch

open_questions: What specific code change in the hysteresis patch caused the agent to enter get_close_to_stair at step 118 instead of the climb_stair passive detection that fired at step 91 in candidate_0? Did the DP12=80 gate block the reinit that would normally trigger passive stair detection during explore mode?
Is mL8ThkuaVTM reliably solvable under the Track 4 baseline (candidate_0 behavior)? A second seed would confirm whether the step-91 passive detection is deterministic.
Does reverting the hysteresis patch and restoring candidate_0 behavior recover the success, or were there confounding episode-level stochastic effects?

causal_graph.hysteresis_patch\u2192changed_stair_appr: candidate_0 enter climb_stair(91-102) passively; candidate_2 enters get_close_to_stair(118-128)→look_for_downstair(129-135) — different code branch triggered
causal_graph.get_close_to_stair_oscillation\u2192elev: 6 reinits in candidate_2 vs 3 in candidate_0; each reinit cycle consumes ~30-50 steps
causal_graph.elevated_reinit_count_plus_500_step_budg: 499 steps consumed by oscillating stair modes and reinit cycles; toilet in feasible 2D region never located

decision_point_attribution.description: Agent enters get_close_to_stair at step 118 (candidate_2) instead of the passive explore path that produced climb_stair at step 91 (candidate_0); this branch point is where the hysteresis patch diverted execution from the successful floor-2 exploration path

contrastive_analysis.note: Single structural difference: candidate_0 uses passive climb_stair(91-102) during explore, then uninterrupted floor-2 exploration; candidate_2 enters active stair approach modes at step 118 and oscillates. Success requires uninterrupted passive stair detection during explore mode; hysteresis patch broke this path by changing the floor-detection gating.

hypothesis_outcome.hypothesis: Floor-switch decisions lack hysteresis, causing the agent to oscillate between floors in short episodes; patching ascent_policy.py to require N consecutive floor-confidence votes before committing a switch will prevent rapid re-triggering.
hypothesis_outcome.assessment: Hypothesis was wrong for this scene: mL8ThkuaVTM was already solved by candidate_0 (not a floor_confusion failure in Track 4), and the hysteresis patch introduced a regression by disrupting the passive climb_stair path, converting a SUCCESS into a FAIL and contributing to an actual SR delta of -0.1 vs predicted +0.1.

XB4GS9ShBRE.root_cause_summary: After successfully climbing stairs at step 198 (floor_step resets to 0, floor 2 entered at step 199), floor 2 presents only 2 frontiers near the stair landing in a navmesh-disconnected component containing the bed room. Candidate_35's room-scale saturation discount was active on floor 2 (visible: 0.9m frontier raw score reduced from 0.107 to 0.021 by the density multiplier), but this is irrelevant — the discount cannot generate new frontiers in navmesh-disconnected areas. The 3 T4_NOQUIT rescues all find an empty frontier pool. Identical behavioral fingerprint across all 14 candidates including candidate_35 confirms every intervention tested is orthogonal to the post-climb navmesh disconnection failure on floor 2.
key_evidence: Step 198: 'climb stair success!!!!'; floor_step=0 at step 198, floor_step=1 at step 199 — stair physically traversed, not stalled
candidate_35 floor-2 scores: 0.107→0.220@2.2m, 0.021→0.446@0.9m — raw score of 0.9m frontier discounted from 0.107 to 0.021 by RSD, confirming discount was active yet floor 2 still only 2 frontiers
Steps 199-215: same 2 frontiers exhausted in ~16 floor_steps; reinit fires at step 215 with 'In all floors, no unexplored stairs or frontiers found, stopping.' — unchanged from all prior candidates
T4_NOQUIT rescues at steps 228 and 241 both find empty frontier pool; episode ends at step 254 — identical to candidates 9/13/32
WARNING: candidates 9/0/13/32/5/2/6/35/4/3/7/8 all share identical mode sequences — 14 total candidates, zero behavioral variation

ruled_out_reasoning.DP12: Frontier exhaustion on floor 2 occurs within ~16 floor_steps, well below any DP12 threshold; no-frontiers path bypasses DP12 entirely
ruled_out_reasoning.DP1: Both floor-2 frontiers score 0.107 raw; proximity boosting cannot generate frontiers in navmesh-disconnected areas
ruled_out_reasoning.LLM_DP7: dp7_empty=0/0; LLM cannot create navmesh paths to disconnected regions
ruled_out_reasoning.DP9: Stair IS physically traversed (climb stair success step 198); carrot distance irrelevant

open_questions: Is the pre-computed geodesic distance finite for this episode? If finite, the 2D 'likely_infeasible' flag is a false-fire and a navigable path exists that the 2D contour check missed.
Are there any floor-2 navmesh nodes adjacent to the bed room reachable from a different stair landing position, or is the disconnection absolute?
Could a frontier augmentation strategy seeding waypoints beyond the stair landing (3D ray-casting or room-boundary detection) create exploration coverage of the disconnected bed region?
candidate_10 showed passive upstairs detection triggering climb_stair at step 231 in q3zU7Yy5E5s — does an analogous passive detection mechanism exist for floor-2 unexplored areas in XB4GS9ShBRE?

decision_point_attribution.description: Floor 2 entered at step 199 with floor_step=1; only 2 low-score frontiers near stair landing; navmesh disconnection between stair landing and bed room makes goal-directed exploration impossible regardless of all subsequent agent decisions or scoring modifications

contrastive_analysis.note: No successes across any candidate. 12 of 14 share identical behavioral fingerprints (candidates 0/2/3/4/5/6/7/8/9/13/32/35). candidate_12 shows a distinct failure (T4_COV_GATE_B blocks stair entirely, steps=121). candidate_10 shows T4_STRETCH firing but stair still climbed successfully. Root cause is navmesh geometry — no harness mechanism targeting any FSM state, frontier scoring, LLM guidance, or zone saturation can generate navigable frontiers in disconnected navmesh components after a successful stair climb.

telemetry_insights.frontier_supply_at_stair_trigger: 5 entries (consistent across candidates); floor 2 has only 2 frontiers post-climb near stair landing

hypothesis_outcome.hypothesis: Room-scale saturation discount (ROOM_GRID=4m, DENSITY_DECAY=0.08, DENSITY_FLOOR=0.20) forces agent to abandon densely-visited spatial zones and route to lowest-density frontier cluster.
hypothesis_outcome.assessment: Discount was active on floor 2 (0.9m frontier raw score dropped 0.107→0.021), but the failure is post-climb navmesh disconnection — only 2 frontiers exist near the stair landing and both lie in a disconnected navmesh component from the bed room. Zone saturation pressure cannot bridge a navmesh gap or generate new frontiers. Identical fingerprint across all 12 matching candidates including candidate_35 confirms zero behavioral effect. XB4GS9ShBRE was not in the RSD target scene list; outcome consistent with prior 12-candidate history.

qyAac8rV8Zk.root_cause_summary: The downstairs stair centroid lies in a navmesh-disconnected component regardless of which centroid is selected: candidate_35 targets [-1.26861111, -8.18583333] (slightly shifted from [-1.22463054, -8.19236453] in candidates 9/13/32, likely due to RSD density effects on stair frontier scoring) with min_dis_to_downstair oscillating 170/177. Reach_stair_centroid is False on every approach attempt. The RSD zone discount is entirely orthogonal to navmesh disconnection — it cannot generate a connected path to the couch floor regardless of which zone receives the largest discount penalty. Steps=243 (up 4 from candidates 13/32 at 239) due to the slight centroid shift requiring marginally longer approach geometry, but terminal failure path is identical.
key_evidence: candidate_35: centroid [-1.26861111, -8.18583333] with min_dis_to_downstair oscillating 170/177 — both values higher than the 159-166 range in candidates 9/13/32, confirming the RSD-shifted centroid is equally disconnected but at a slightly greater navmesh distance
Two disable events: first at min_dis=170, second at min_dis=177 — both Reach_stair_centroid=False; same two-disable cycle as prior candidates
'Environment 0: Navigating downstairs to unexplored floor' fires after first disable (step ~N), confirming stair re-enabled and second approach initiated — identical pattern to candidates 13/32
steps=243 (vs 239 for candidates 13/32, vs 202 for candidate_9) — 4-step increase from shifted centroid approach geometry, not from RSD discovering a new path
dp7_empty=0/0, llm_parse_rate=1.00 — LLM functioning normally but cannot nominate a navmesh-connected alternative because none exists

Does pathfinder.find_path() return path_found=True by snapping to a nearby navmesh node for both centroids [-1.22,-8.19] and [-1.26,-8.18]? If yes, the pathfinder API is fundamentally unsuitable for disconnection detection and island_radius() or is_navigable() with tight tolerance is required.

A step-budget (T=20) or Reach_stair_centroid=False count (N=5) applied specifically to get_close_to_stair mode — would this exit the 75-step stall before step 200, when intrafloor frontier supply might still be nonzero?

candidate_10's T4_STRETCH fired correctly (stretch=3.94) but intrafloor pool was already empty — at what step does the intrafloor frontier pool exhaust? Is it before or after get_close_to_stair entry at step 164?

hypothesis_outcome.hypothesis: Room-scale saturation discount forces agent out of densely-visited zones (target scene: qyAac8rV8Zk). Predicted: agent routes to lowest-density frontier cluster, eliminating cycling in fixed spatial region.
hypothesis_outcome.assessment: Refuted. The RSD discount shifted centroid selection by ~0.04m ([-1.22,-8.19] to [-1.26,-8.18]) and added 4 steps (239→243), but both centroids are in the same disconnected navmesh component. The couch floor failure in qyAac8rV8Zk is not a zone-cycling problem — it is a navmesh topology problem where the only detected stair entry is physically unreachable. Zone saturation pressure is irrelevant when the binding constraint is navmesh disconnection, not frontier ranking.

The room-scale saturation discount is structurally orthogonal to navmesh disconnection failures.

open_questions: Is the pre-computed geodesic distance finite for q3zU7Yy5E5s? If yes, the navmesh disconnection is a detection/snapping failure rather than a true topology failure, and snap_point() resolution may solve it.

candidate_10 showed passive upstairs detection at step 231 triggering successful stair climb (Reach_stair_centroid: True, climb stair success at step 239), but floor 2 was also exhausted. Does this suggest the couch is on a third floor, or that the floor-2 exploration was insufficient to find it?

A step-budget (T=25) or Reach_stair_centroid=False count (N=5) applied specifically to get_close_to_stair: would this exit the stall before ~step 210, when there might still be intrafloor frontier coverage?

Five centroids tested in the [-1.3 to -2.1, 3.3 to 3.7] region all show Reach_stair_centroid=False — is there any navmesh-connected node in the stairwell, or is the entire stair structure geometry not reflected in the navmesh?

decision_point_attribution.description: Agent enters look_down→get_close_to_stair sequence at step 179 targeting disconnected centroid [-2.12027027, 3.27567568]; no harness mechanism tested across 14 candidates (including candidate_35 RSD) targets get_close_to_stair mode directly with an infeasibility exit condition; episode budget consumed in the stall with no fallback; this is the correct injection point for all future interventions

contrastive_analysis.note: No successes across any candidate or track. 9 of 14 candidates share identical fingerprints (0/3/6/7/8/9/13/32/35, steps=381). candidate_4 shows 2-step variation; candidate_2 shows 3-disable trajectory (+19 steps); candidate_5 shows false_positive from extended look_for_downstair; candidate_10 shows passive stair climb to a different floor (also exhausted); candidate_12 shows coverage-gated intrafloor-only path. All 14 converge to the same terminal state: no accessible floor containing the couch. Candidate_35 definitively rules out room-scale zone saturation as a lever, joining 13 prior failed mechanisms. The irreducible bottleneck is area-wide navmesh disconnection of the entire stairwell region.

telemetry_insights.llm_parse_rate_trend: stable (1.00 all candidates; dp7_empty=0/1 for candidates 0/3/4/6/7/8/9/13/32/35; candidate_2 dp7_empty=0/2 due to extra re-approach cycles)
telemetry_insights.frontier_supply_at_stair_trigger: 5 entries (latest; consistent); 0 intrafloor frontiers when disable fires — confirmed by candidate_3/9/12 immediate terminal states

hypothesis_outcome.hypothesis: Room-scale saturation discount forces agent out of densely-visited zones (target scene: q3zU7Yy5E5s). Predicted: agent abandons densely-visited spatial zones after ~12 visits and routes to lowest-density frontier cluster.
hypothesis_outcome.assessment: Fully refuted by identical behavioral fingerprint (steps=381, same centroid) to candidates 0/3/6/7/8/9/13/32. The RSD mechanism was computationally active (evidenced by reduced iteration speed 48-58 it/s vs 62-65 it/s) but produced zero change in which stair centroid was targeted, how many steps were wasted, or what the terminal failure state was. q3zU7Yy5E5s fails due to area-wide navmesh disconnection of the stairwell, not due to zone-cycling in any room — the zone saturation hypothesis does not address the correct failure mechanism for this scene.

p53SfW6mjZe.root_cause_summary: Candidate_35 introduced a regression on this previously reliably-solved scene (12/13 prior successes). The room-scale saturation discount reduced the BLIP-2-enhanced score of the TV-containing frontier by step 78 — 19 steps before the navigate trigger that fires in all 12 successful candidates (~step 97-98). The agent entered navigate mode at step 79 targeting a different low-density frontier instead, initiating a 100-step detour (navigate 79-178) away from the TV area. After re-entering explore at step 179, all zones are maximally discounted and the agent cycles targeting a single far frontier [2.675, -10.375] with scores ~0.034 for the remaining 320 steps, eventually calling a false-positive STOP at step 499. The RSD mechanism correctly identified zone saturation but lacked any protection for high-value frontiers whose genuine BLIP-2 signal was above background.
key_evidence: Successful candidates (0/2/3/4/6/7/8/9/10/13/32): explore(21-97)→navigate(98-121/122)→SUCCESS — TV frontier reached at floor_step ~97-98 with sufficient proximity boost
candidate_35: explore terminates at step 78 (vs 97 in successes), navigate(79-178) selected — 19 steps early departure and 100-step detour to non-TV area
Late-episode candidate_35 frontier scores (steps ~420-493): 0.034@4.7-5.3m and 0.025@5.3-6.6m repeatedly targeting frontier [2.675, -10.375] — all zones at DENSITY_FLOOR, no recovery

candidate_35 dp7_empty=0/2 vs dp7_empty=0/0 in successful candidates — 2 LLM invocations during the extended episode vs none in successful runs, indicating divergent navigation requiring LLM arbitration
steps=499 (full budget exhausted) vs 109-122 in successes — 4x step count confirms complete navigation efficiency collapse
Failure class: false_positive — agent called STOP on a non-TV object, confirming TV was never located on the correct path

open_questions: At step 78-79, was the TV-containing frontier scored below the competing frontier due to RSD discount alone, or did the LLM explicitly recommend the non-TV frontier? dp7_empty=0/2 confirms 2 LLM calls were made — were they at steps 79 and 179?

What is the density count for the TV-containing zone at step 78? If the agent navigated near it ~12 times (DENSITY_FLOOR threshold at DENSITY_DECAY=0.08), the floor discount would be maximally applied regardless of BLIP-2 score.
Does a BLIP-2 raw score threshold (e.g., never apply RSD to frontiers with raw score > 0.10) prevent the regression while still breaking exploration dead-ends in the 4 target scenes?

Is [2.675, -10.375] — the frontier the agent cyclically targets in the late episode — on a different floor or a dead-end corridor? The near-constant distance oscillation (4.7-5.3m) without arrival suggests it is either in a low-navmesh-density area or the navigate planner is oscillating without convergence.

causal_graph.RSD_visits_accumulated_in_TV_zone_by_ste: Explore steps 21-78 (~57 steps) accumulated density in TV-containing 4m grid cell; RSD discount reduced enhanced score below DENSITY_FLOOR × raw_BLIP2, falling below a competing low-density cell's score

causal_graph.240_steps_cycling_on_low_score_frontier\: Agent eventually observes non-TV object with detection score sufficient for STOP call; actual TV never located

decision_point_attribution.description: Agent departs explore mode at step 78 and enters navigate mode at step 79 targeting a non-TV frontier — in all 12 successful candidates navigate mode begins at steps 95-109 targeting the TV frontier. The RSD discount on the TV-containing zone at step 78 caused the scoring inversion that selected the wrong target, making this the sole point of divergence from the success trajectory.

contrastive_analysis.note: 12 successes share explore(21-97)→navigate(98-121/122)→SUCCESS. Both failures deviate from this path: candidate_5 at step 119 via stair FSM extension, candidate_35 at step 79 via RSD deflection. The 12-success structural commonality is that explore mode persists to step 94-97 before navigate trigger, suggesting the TV frontier only accumulates sufficient proximity-boosted score after ~75 floor_steps of exploration building up the distance boost from approach. The RSD discount short-circuits this accumulation by suppressing the frontier score below a competing zone's score before the proximity boost reaches trigger level.

telemetry_insights.llm_parse_rate_trend: stable (1.00); but candidate_35 shows 2 LLM calls (dp7_empty=0/2) vs 0 LLM calls in successful candidates (dp7_empty=0/0) — extra invocations are consequence of divergent navigation path, not a cause

hypothesis_outcome.hypothesis: p53SfW6mjZe was NOT in the RSD target scene list (target: mL8ThkuaVTM, XB4GS9ShBRE, qyAac8rV8Zk, q3zU7Yy5E5s). candidate_35 regressed this scene from SUCCESS to FAIL.
hypothesis_outcome.assessment: Unintended regression. The RSD mechanism deflected the agent from the TV-containing zone before the proximity boost could raise its score to navigate-triggering level; the 100-step detour to a non-TV area consumed the step budget and eliminated all further opportunities to find the TV. This is the sole contributor to candidate_35's overall SR drop from 0.7 (candidate_32) to 0.6. The failure exposes a fundamental RSD design flaw: DENSITY_DECAY=0.08 and DENSITY_FLOOR=0.20 are aggressive enough to suppress genuine high-value frontiers in scenes where the target object requires sustained exploration of a single spatial zone before detection, not just cycling behavior.
